title: TVS Travel Network — live segment
text: roses and poetry. Famous Persian poets once lived here and created works of great beauty. Today their mausoleums are visited with much reverence, such as the Huffy's tomb. This, the Paradise Garden, is one of the city's many parks. Nazar al-Mok had a three-story palace built here. It's now a botanical garden. Ungols left Shiraz undamaged, but the Afghans conquered, destroyed and looted the city. And finally followed a curd, Karim Khanzand. He built a citadel that protected this trading center. The future capital of his empire and a magnificent city of wealth was born. With its many domed rooms, the Vakil Bathas was built in around 1776 AD with lifelike scenes that are simulated here and the comfort of an inviting swimming complex is conveyed. The Vakil complex also contains a mosque that was built at the same time as the Bazaar. The Vakil Bazaar was considered to be the most beautiful in Persia. Its main street is about 800 meters long. From 1871, the Bazaar and its warehouses served as a center of trade for a hundred years. A traditional tea house surprises. The Shah Shirakh Mosulium contains the remains of the brother of Imam Reza. The King of light died in Shiraz and is revered as a saint. The ancient Friday Mosque is entered through the Mosulium. The Nazir Almolk Mosque is known as the Rose Mosque. The winter prayer hall shines out in the light of stained glass windows. The old city palace of the Kavam Almolk family is now a museum. In a small garden, palace guests were received and escorted via steps to an open portico. The Madresikan is the most well-known traditional university in Shiraz. The Persian orange garden of Delgosia also attracts with its old buildings. Valuable exhibits are displayed in splendid rooms. Exhibits that indicate the amazing wealth of their original owners. Nearby is a garden that contains the Mosulium of the poet Sadi. This, the center of Persian literature, became known far beyond the national border. Its heire was, however, only short-lived, as well as that of the Zan dynasty. Lotvali Khan was assassinated and Tehran became the capital of the Qajaran Empire. Closed by is Persepolis, city of the Persians. Although Darius the Great had two capital cities, Pasagarai and Susa. He wanted to present to the world a third impressive city. During several decades of construction the glorious total work of Persian Achaemenid art was achieved. The gate of all nations, a 70 meter long relief on the eastern stairway of the portico depicts a number of envoys of the 28 nations of the Achaemenid Empire. Babylonians, Aromites, Armenians, Ethiopians, Syrians, Indians and many more. In national clothing and head attire, mythical creatures in stones such as lion griffons and horse heads lie scattered around. They once supported the roofs of the various halls. Darius was succeeded by his son Xerxes who had further palaces built as well as the remarkable Hall of a hundred columns. For 58 years Ionic stone masons, Babylonian brickmakers, Median and Egyptian goldsmiths worked in Persepolis. They created what was an indelible symbol of the Achaemenid Empire. The highest level contained the royal residences with living rooms of fire temple and harem. Darius, Xerxes and Artaxes are the known kings who left the magnificent buildings in Persepolis and Susa. This is the most important architectural legacy of the first Persian Empire which had its heyday between the 6th and 4th centuries BC. Around four kilometers away are vertical rock walls. Here the first Achaemenid kings had their gravesites cut in stone to which were added huge grave reliefs. The sacenids added rock tomb reliefs below the gravesite with royal martial motifs. Darius the first was the first king to have his tomb built into the rock. The site became known as Nakshirostam, portrait of Rostam. The grave of Kiros is an impressive site. In the vast Dashtimahab plateau are the ruins of Pasagarai, the first royal Achaemenid city of residence founded by Shah Kiros II. Although the Achaemenid made Hamadan one of its summer residences, they also built their headquarters here. A sprawling city system in the form of a large camp with numerous spread out buildings. On this plateau history is still alive. It was here that Iranian tribes settled following centuries of wandering. They founded a small kingdom under their first ruler Achaemenis. There was a residential palace, audience hall and gatehouse, but now only ruins. The Paradeasos Garden is no longer visible. Esfahan, half the world. With its historic center, this oasis city situated on the banks of the Zayandehru River is a gem of ancient Persia and one of the most beautiful cities in the Islamic world. Its captivating and atmospheric Friday mosque attracts many visitors throughout the year. In the center of the historic city is the center of the modest Piber Halls Mosque which is thought to have been built above a fire temple. Lush flowering gardens with ponds welcome travelers following their strenuous caravan journeys. With garden palaces such as the Kakaketul Sotun, where the Safavid rulers received their guests, in the Nightingale Garden is the palace of eight paradises which was used as a harine, believed to have been occupied by the Shah's favorite eight wives. Following the death of Shahrabaz, around 30,000 Christian Armenians settled in the Zulfur district where they built the Saviour Cathedral is also a monastery with commemorative plaques and gravestones. A special attraction is the admirable Kaha Bridge, the most beautiful in the city, 132 meters long, 12 meters wide and with 23 arches. The grandeur of Islamic architecture is reflected in Imam Square, the central square of the Safavid era known as the image of the world. It's seven times larger than the Piazza Samarko in Venice. To the south the square is lined by a domed structure which for some years has been known as the Imam Hormani Mosque, perfectly aligned towards Mecca. It is of breathtaking proportions and contains the oriental splendor of precious seven-colored glazed half-rung tiles. However, Shahrabaz did not live to see its completion. The Arikapu Gate Palace is located in the middle of the western side of the Mayan. Ornate, Stucco and Ingles adorn a stand supported by columns with canopy from where the king could have an overview of sporting competitions and parades. Opposite is the Lotofala Mosque whose dome despite its modest dimensions is a masterpiece. It is possibly the most perfect faeze dome in the world both in color and pattern design. The pageantry portal in the north of the square leads to the Caisserie, the imperial bazaar. Here there are coppersmiths, traditional fabric printers and spice merchants in addition to various jewelry designers. The niches of fruit traders are next to those of cloth merchants, who in their care maynard king of kings once appreciated the charms of this desert settlement. Esfahan possessed many mosques and caravans arise. Since 1979 the Abazi family has owned the most beautiful hotel in Iran, a traditional place to stay as in the past. And even today magnificent furnishings bring the fantasy of the thousand and one nights to life. Esfahan is an architectural work of art. We return to western Iran. Before arriving in Hamadan there's another surprise in store. In 2009 during road works in the remote hills the entrance to an underground cave system was discovered. At first five large rooms then more or and more connecting passages. And now another 70 underground caves with dwellings seven stories high. Why did their inhabitants choose to live beneath the earth's surface after carrying out painstaking excavation work with primitive tools? It is not known who lived here. Above ground in Hamadan were more than 1,000 villages. Vakeneh depicts the typical construction and lifestyle of those times. Simple houses are mostly built of stone which was used as protection from the severe weather conditions. Strong, durable, and resistant to all kinds of extreme weather. The narrow streets provided shelter from freezing winter storms at the life-sapping heat of the summer. It's now a place of Islamic cultural heritage and a popular tourist destination. Fertile farmlands surrounds each village. Watered by various sources of the Azar food region, invasions, battles, and revolutions always dictated the numerous great empires which developed on Persian soil. The Kingdom of Medea was the first great empire with Hamadan as its capital. Today, the splendor of the revered tombs of the religious leaders of more recent times such as the Imam Zareh by Ahmad and Abdullah. The deep religious faith of the people has been well preserved here. A grave tower that dates back to Seljuk times has its charm. Completely built of fired clay bricks, its interior with stucco decorations. It was the burial place of the Alavi family who determined the history of the city for around 200 years. A more recent, very modern tomb for the mystic poet Baba Tahir has become a popular destination for both young and old alike. A stone sarcophagus is surrounded by his poems. On a nearby hill is the excavation site of the city's oldest original settlement that dates back to the time before the Medea founded their capital, Hamadan. The remains of the walls of buildings and streets have been excavated. And objects found here are exhibited in the museum. Although precious gold and silver artifacts are kept at Tehran's National Museum. Armenian churches indicate that there was once a great Armenian Christian community here. Also buildings such as the Nazari house from the Qadja period. It was a time when Europe influenced many aspects of design. The oldest building in the city is an old pagoda. The mausoleum of Esther and Mordechai. The most important Jewish place of pilgrimage on Iranian soil. And a famous pre-Islamic stone lion even today seems to stand guard for the city's senior citizens. The tomb of Ibn Sina is a reminder of the extraordinary physician, philosopher and poet, who became famous as the Doctor of Doctors. At the bottom of the Alavad Masif are various ancient rock inscriptions. The only visible remains that date back to the Achaemenid period. For the Hamadanis, this place is also a popular destination. This country possesses many faces and is proud of its history and the ancient and cultural treasures of Persia. Iran is still a land of desert, palaces and mosques. A country that is timeless, captivating and historic. Located in the middle of Europe, Slovakia has a long and varied history. It contains Europe's youngest capital city and also its highest mountain range as well as cultural masterpieces and unspoiled nature. Bratislava, located in the heart of Europe and the capital of Slovakia, a city with a colorful history. Surrounded by the city hall and several palaces, it's like being taken back in time into a rich and glorious past. At the beginning of the 14th century, this then fortress town had around 6,000 inhabitants. Today, the baroque ceremonial rooms in the Old Town Hall are open to the public and there is also a museum. It was the region's first city and has a typical town hall. Directly behind the Old City Hall is the primatial palace that was built in 1781. It's classicism in its purest form. The Jesuit church was built in 1638 as a Protestant church. The extensive Franciscan palace adjoins the main square and leads to a monastery and church of the Franciscans. The city's oldest well-preserved sacred building. The narrow Mikalska leads gently uphill and ends at a tower gate that opens to the lover's bridge. Long pedestrian boulevard leads to the Slovakian National Theater, which was built in neo-Renaissance style in 1886. A visit to the old Nicola cemetery is like a journey back to the time of the Danube monarchy. Returning to the city, we visit the residential palace, which was built as a garden palace for Anton Grusselkovic. Behind the palace, there's a large garden. The Gothic St. Martin's Cathedral is a huge old fortress church that was once adjacent to the city walls. Between the 16th and 18th centuries, 11 Hungarian kings and eight royal wives were crowned in the city's Catholic Cathedral. The cathedral is regarded as the city's greatest religious building. Leading from the cathedral is Kapitulska Street, and in a small side alley is the monastery of Clarissus Church. The mendicant orders were forbidden to construct a church tower along the church's main axis. In the northern section of the old town that once lay directly in front of the city wall, the Trinitarian Order built one of the most beautiful Baroque churches in the city. The vast number of ornate altars and sacred sculptures make this house of God into a veritable religious museum. Next, and overlooking the castle, the Kapuchin Order built a church consecrated to St. Stephen of Hungary.
